response.answer:
Shrimp are omnivorous creatures, meaning they can consume both plant and animal matter. Their diet typically includes:

1. Plankton: Both phytoplankton (tiny plants) and zooplankton (tiny animals).
2. Small crustaceans: Such as copepods, amphipods, and other small shrimp species.
3. Organic debris: Shrimp will scavenge for dead plant and animal matter on the ocean floor.
4. Algae: Some species of shrimp may consume algae growing in their environment.

In captivity or aquaculture settings, shrimp are often fed commercial shrimp pellets that mimic their natural diet. These pellets contain a mix of proteins, carbohydrates, vitamins, and minerals to ensure balanced nutrition for the shrimp.

The specific dietary needs can vary slightly depending on the species of shrimp, with some preferring more plant-based diets while others may have a stronger inclination towards animal matter.

treatments.treatmentTitle: Callidosoma cassiculophylla Bernardi & Wohltmann & Ferreira 2017, sp. nov.
treatments.articleTitle: A novel symbiotic relationship between mites and recluse spiders (Sicariidae: Loxosceles), with a description of a new Callidosoma species (Trombidiformes: Erythraeidae)
treatments.articleAuthor: Bernardi, Leopoldo Ferreira De Oliveira, Wohltmann, Andreas & Ferreira, Rodrigo Lopes
treatments.speciesDesc: The answer to your question about the relationship between Loxosceles and C. cassiculophylla sp. nov. is a case of "kleptoparasitism". This means that the mite (C. cassiculophylla sp. nov.) is consuming prey captured by other species (Loxosceles) without displaying any aggressive behavior, which is unusual for arachnids and insects.

images.captionText: FIGURE 1. Details of a Callidosoma cassiculophylla sp. nov., adult, associated with webs of Loxosceles spiders in Brazilian caves. A: Dorsal aspect; B: Details of the frontal region of the crista metopica; C: Details of the front dorsal region of the podosoma; D: Details of the ventral setae of idiosoma; E: Details of the dorsal setae of idiosoma.
FIGURE 2. Callidosoma cassiculophylla sp. nov., adult, habitus dorsal view. ap = anal opening, ch = chelicera, cm = crista metopica, gp = genital opening.

FIGURE 3. Callidosoma cassiculophylla sp. nov., adult. A: Ventral view of palp tarsus; B: Dorsal view of palp; C: Crista metopica. AL = anterior setae, ASens = anterior sensilla, PSens = posterior sensilla; D; Dorsal body setae. L = lateral view, D = dorsal view, V = ventral view.
FIGURE 4. Callidosoma cassiculophylla sp. nov., adult, scanning electron micrographs. Dorsal body setae (A) and ventral body setae (B).
FIGURE 5. Callidosoma cassiculophylla sp. nov., adult. Lateral view of tarsus I. Only specialized setae shown, DS = normal dorsal seta, VS = normal ventral seta.
FIGURE 7. Callidosoma cassiculophylla sp. nov., adult (A – D). Mites associated with Loxosceles sp. webs in caves of Varzelândia, Minas Gerais, Brazil.

FIGURE 8. Callidosoma cassiculophylla sp. nov., adult. Mites associated with webs of Loxosceles variegata, Lapa Dágua cave, in Lassance, Minas Gerais, Brazil. A: Mites found dispersed throughout the web; B, C and D: Callidosoma cassiculophylla sp. nov. feeding on Hypena sp. beside a spider after the capture of its prey, Cazanga Cave (Arcos, Minas Gerais, Brazil).

treatments.articleTitle: Taxonomic review of Hadromerida (Porifera, Demospongiae) from British Columbia, Canada, and adjacent waters, with the description of nine new species
treatments.articleAuthor: Austin, William C., Ott, Bruce S., Reiswig, Henry M., Romagosa, Paula & G, Neil
treatments.speciesDesc: Suberites latus, also known as the "smooth surface" or "flat bottom" sponge, is a species of marine sponge that belongs to the genus Suberites. It is characterized by its smooth, flat body form, which can range in size from thin crusts on snail shells to massive forms up to 10 cm long and 4 cm high. The sponge's surface is typically covered with oscula (small, finger-like structures) that are flush with the surface, and it contains one hermit crab (Pagurus stevensae Hart, 1971). Suberites latus is commonly found in shallow waters, particularly in areas with soft or unconsolidated sediment, such as beaches, bays, and estuaries. It is a cosmopolitan species, meaning it can be found in various parts of the world's oceans, including the North Pacific, South Pacific, and Mediterranean Sea.

images.captionText: FIGURE 23. Suberites latus Lambe, 1893. A, B, typical smooth surface morph, scale bar approx. 1 cm; C, rough surface morph, scale bar approx. 1 cm; D, section normal to surface, scale bar 1 mm; E, curved tylostyle; F, straight tylostyle; G, tylostrongyle; H, I, fat and thin acanthose centrotylote strongyles, scale bars E – I, 10 µm.

treatments.articleTitle: A Revision of the Genus Poltys in Australasia (Araneae: Araneidae)

Potamonautes nheenae, a new species of freshwater crab from Gabon, Central Africa, with a description of the first known male of Erimetopus brazzae (A. Milne-Edwards, 1886) from the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Central Africa (Brachyura: Potamoidea: Potamonautidae)
treatments.speciesDesc: Erimetopus brazzae, also known as Brazza's crab, is a species of freshwater crab found in the Lower Congo River basin in the Republic of the Congo and the Democratic Republic of Congo. It has a subhexagonal/rounded carapace outline, large epibranchial teeth, and a slender cheliped with pointed teeth. The species is characterized by its ability to live in muddy ground near river banks, where it can be found under stones, boardwalks, or tangled in water plants.

images.captionText: FIGURE 6. Erimetopus brazzae (A. Milne-Edwards, 1886). Subadult male, CW 11 mm, from Kinganga, D. R. Congo (MCZ 67421). A. Carapace, dorsal view. B. thoracic sternum and pleon. C. G 1 ventral view. D. G 1 dorsal view. E. G 2 ventral view. F. merus, carpus of right cheliped, dorsal view. Scale bar: 3.0 mm (A, B, F), 1.4 mm (C – E).

treatments.treatmentTitle: Bairdiella goeldi Marceniuk & Molina & Caires & Rotundo & Wosiacki & Oliveira 2019, sp. nov.
treatments.articleTitle: Revision of Bairdiella (Sciaenidae: Perciformes) from the western South Atlantic, with insights into its diversity and biogeography
treatments.articleAuthor: Marceniuk, Alexandre Pires, Molina, Eduardo Garcia, Caires, Rodrigo Antunes, Rotundo, Matheus Marcos, Wosiacki, Wolmar Benjamin & Oliveira, Claudio
treatments.speciesDesc:
The text describes a new species of fish, Bairdiella goeldi sp. nov., which is a type of marine or estuarine fish found in Brazil. The species was discovered by a team of researchers from the Museu Paraense Emílio Goeldi and other institutions.

Here are some key points about the species:

* It is a relatively rare species, with no commercial importance in Brazil.
* It has a distinctive appearance, with grayish above the lateral line, silvery below, and blackish dorsal, anal, and caudal fins.
* The species is found on the Atlantic coast of Brazil, from at least the equatorial northern state of Pará to Santa Catarina.
* It is abundant in estuarine waters on sandy or muddy bottoms on the northern and southeastern coasts of Brazil.
* The species has no commercial importance in Brazil, but it can be incidentally caught as bycatch in bottom trawls, gillnets, and seines.

The species was named after the Goeldi Museum (Museu Paraense Emílio Goeldi) in Belém, Pará, which supported taxonomic research on marine and estuarine fish of Brazil.

treatments.treatmentTitle: Perioculodes talboti Hughes & Lowry 2009, sp. nov.
treatments.articleAuthor: Hughes, L. E. & Lowry, J. K.
treatments.speciesDesc: Perioculodes talboti is a species of oedicerotid crustacean, specifically a figid. It was described by K.H. Barnard in 1931 and is named after Frank Talbot, the founder of the Lizard Island Research Station. The species has been found to inhabit shallow waters with coarse to fine sand bottoms during the day and at night, swimming in the water column.

images.captionText: FIGURE 5. Perioculodes talboti sp. nov., holotype, female, 4.1 mm, AM P78151, First Lagoon, One Tree Island, Great Barrier Reef.
FIGURE 6. Perioculodes talboti sp. nov., holotype, female, 4.1 mm, AM P78151, First Lagoon, One Tree Island, Great Barrier Reef.

detectBinomens.sql: SELECT DISTINCT binomen FROM binomens WHERE LOWER(binomen) IN ('what do','do shrimp','shrimp eat')

detectTreatmentAuthors.sql: SELECT DISTINCT treatmentAuthor FROM treatmentAuthors ta JOIN treatments t ON ta.treatments_id = t.id WHERE LOWER(treatmentAuthor) IN ('what do','do shrimp','shrimp eat')

fts.sql: SELECT DISTINCT t.id AS treatments_id, fts.rank AS rank FROM treatmentsFts fts JOIN treatment_chunks tc ON fts.rowid = tc.id JOIN treatments t ON tc.treatments_id = t.id WHERE fts.fulltext MATCH 'What OR do OR shrimp OR eat' ORDER BY rank LIMIT 100
